claim: Study based on 106 participants who own or work for a licensed cannabis cultivation operation
context: Results are based on 106 participants who own or work for a licensed operation that cultivates cannabis for sale.
notes: Study conducted by Readex Research on behalf of Cannabis Business Times

claim: Margin of error is approximately plus or minus 9.5 percentage points at 95% confidence level
context: The margin of error for percentages based on the 106 participants who indicated they own or work for a cultivation operation that grows cannabis for sale is approximately ±9.5 percentage points at the 95% confidence level.

claim: Optimal drying room conditions are 60 to 74 degrees F and 40% to 60% humidity
context: "For drying room conditions, we found the optimal window is 60 to 74 degrees F and 40% to 60% humidity," he says.
notes: Lower bound of optimal range per Dr. Markus Roggen research

claim: Optimal drying time is 10 to 14 days to preserve terpenes
context: "We found 10 to 14 days worked well for us to preserve terpenes but to free the room for the next harvest."
notes: Per Dr. Markus Roggen research at OutCo; lower bound of range

claim: 7% of cultivators prefer variable temperature in drying rooms
context: preferring "variable" conditions for both temperature (7%) and humidity (11%).

claim: 11% of cultivators prefer variable humidity in drying rooms
context: preferring "variable" conditions for both temperature (7%) and humidity (11%).

claim: Quest has been active in the cannabis market for more than 10 years
context: Having been active in the cannabis market for more than 10 years
notes: Per Clifton Tomasini, Quest President

claim: CBT has conducted exclusive research reports for the past five years
context: For the past five years, Cannabis Business Times' exclusive research reports have focused on investigating and benchmarking various aspects of cultivation

claim: Rhode Island has 7 licensed Compassion Centers/Hybrid Cannabis Retailers
context: Licensed Compassion Centers/Hybrid Cannabis Retailers 7
notes: Two additional licenses pending (one in Zone 1, one in Zone 3)

claim: Rhode Island has 57 licensed cultivators
context: Licensed Cultivators 57

claim: Rhode Island authorized 24 new adult-use cannabis retail licenses across six geographic zones
context: the Commission is authorized to license up to 24 new retail-only establishments statewide, divided equally across six geographic zones
notes: Application portal opened September 12, 2025; closed December 29, 2025

claim: Rhode Island has 9 hemp growers/handlers, 18 hemp distributors, and 159 hemp retailers licensed
context: Hemp Growers/Handlers 9 Hemp Distributers 18 Hemp Retailers 159

table_data.table_title: Rhode Island Hemp Licensing Data

notes: Total of 186 hemp licenses

claim: Rhode Island Cannabis Control Commission received 381 public comments during APA rulemaking process and finalized 180 pages of regulatory language
context: hosting a public hearing and carefully reviewing 381 public comments. These contributions informed the final regulations, encompassing 180 pages of regulatory language
notes: Commission became standalone state agency on May 1, 2025

claim: Rhode Island Cannabis Control Commission became a standalone state agency on May 1, 2025
context: final regulations, encompassing 180 pages of regulatory language, that officially established the Commission as a standalone state agency on May 1, 2025
notes: Rhode Island Cannabis Act signed May 25, 2022; commissioners confirmed June 13, 2023

claim: Rhode Island received approximately 220 interested parties for Social Equity Applicant Status Certification
context: We received around 220 interested parties and are currently reviewing submissions
notes: Social Equity Program includes 6 reserved Social Equity Retail Licenses

claim: Rhode Island reserved 6 Social Equity Retail Licenses as part of the adult-use cannabis expansion
context: Applying for certification is a prerequisite for those seeking one of the six reserved Social Equity Retail Licenses

claim: Rhode Island CCC tracked 30 legislative bills with cannabis, hemp, or public health policy implications
context: The Commission remained actively engaged with the legislative process, tracking 30 bills with potential implications for cannabis, hemp, and public health policy in Rhode Island

claim: Rhode Island transferred regulatory authority of hemp and hemp-derived products from Department of Business Regulation to the Cannabis Control Commission in FY2026 budget
context: the Fiscal Year 2026 state budget (Article 3, Section 1) brought a major structural change by transferring regulatory authority of hemp and hemp-derived products from the Department of Business Regulation to the Commission
notes: Includes growing, production, distribution, sale, licensing, enforcement, and public health standards

claim: Cannabis prohibition has created disparate harms especially for BIPOC largely through arrest disparities for possession and their downstream effects
context: Cannabis prohibition has created disparate harms—especially for Black, Indigenous, and People of Color ("BIPOC")—largely through arrest disparities for possession and their downstream effects.
notes: Abstract finding; paper published in Boston University Law Review Vol. 101:1003 (2021)

claim: Sealing or expunging cannabis possession convictions could affect many more BIPOC—possibly close to two orders of magnitude more—than prioritizing individuals for entrepreneurship or employment in the cannabis industry
context: sealing or expunging cannabis possession convictions could affect many more BIPOC—possibly close to two orders of magnitude more—than prioritizing these individuals for entrepreneurship or employment opportunities in the cannabis industry.
notes: Key argument of the paper; 'two orders of magnitude' = roughly 100x difference in scale

claim: Non-Hispanic Blacks represent 13% of past-year cannabis users but 23% of drug arrests reported by those users, and make 24% of cannabis purchases
context: Non-Hispanic blacks represent 13% of past-year cannabis users vs. 23% of drug arrests reported by those users, but they report making 24% of the buys. Thus, some of their higher arrest rate may be a consequence of their purchase patterns.
notes: Source: Burns, Caulkins, Everingham & Kilmer (2013), Frontiers Psychiatry

claim: Among 2,756 officers studied, five (0.5%) appeared to be stopping a significantly larger fraction of Black pedestrians and ten were stopping an excessive fraction of Hispanic pedestrians
context: After using a doubly robust benchmark construction to compare the racial distribution of the stops of 2,756 officers, we found five officers who appeared to be stopping a significantly larger fraction of black pedestrians and 10 officers stopping an excessive fraction of Hispanic pedestrians when compared with stops other officers made at the same times and places. Those fifteen are just 0.5% of the officers in the sample.
notes: Source: Ridgeway & MacDonald (2009), J. Am. Stat. Ass'n

claim: Adult cannabis arrest rate decreased by 131.28 per 100,000 population after decriminalization and by 168.50 per 100,000 after legalization
context: The adult arrest rate decreased by 131.28 (95% CI, 106.23-154.21) per 100 000 population after the implementation of decriminalization and 168.50 (95% CI, 158.64-229.65) per 100 000 population after the implementation of legalization.
notes: Source: Plunk et al. (2019), JAMA Pediatrics; study of 38 states 2000-2016; 4 legalized, 7 decriminalized

claim: Youth cannabis arrest rate decreased by 60 per 100,000 population after decriminalization but did not significantly change after legalization
context: The arrest rate for youth decreased by 60 (95% CI, 42-75) per 100 000 population after decriminalization but did not significantly change after legalization in a state (7 per 100 000 population; 95% CI, −15 to 30).
notes: Source: Plunk et al. (2019), JAMA Pediatrics

claim: In Washington State, marijuana arrest rates for both African American and White adults decreased significantly with legalization, but relative disparities for African Americans of legal age increased
context: Marijuana arrest rates among both African American and White adults decreased significantly with legalization of possession, and stayed at a dramatically lower rate after the marijuana retail market opened. However, relative disparities in marijuana arrest rates for African Americans increased for those of legal age, and remained unchanged for younger adults.
notes: Source: Firth et al. (2019), Substance Use & Misuse

claim: In Oregon, adult cannabis legalization was associated with increased juvenile cannabis allegations; relative disparities decreased for Black youth but remained unchanged for American Indian/Alaska Native youth
context: Adult cannabis legalization in Oregon was associated with increased juvenile cannabis allegations; increases are not explained by changes in underage cannabis use. Relative disparities decreased for black youth but remained unchanged for American Indian/Alaska Native youth.
notes: Source: Firth et al. (2020), Am. J. Preventive Med.

claim: Illinois governor granted pardons to more than 11,000 individuals convicted of possessing less than thirty grams of cannabis on the eve of legalization
context: The day before legalization went into effect in Illinois, the governor granted pardons to more than 11,000 individuals who had been convicted of possessing less than thirty grams of cannabis; this pardon effectively expunged the offense from their criminal records.
notes: Source: Chicago Tribune, Jan. 1, 2020

claim: An estimated 10,000 cannabis businesses that touch the product existed in 2017 in the legal market
context: one estimate put the total number of cannabis businesses that touch the product (as opposed to ancillary services like cannabis marketing firms) close to 10,000 in 2017.
notes: Source: CNN Money, Jan. 31, 2018

claim: 81% of cannabis businesses are owned by those who identify as White, while Asian, Black, and Hispanic/Latinx owners represent 2.4%, 4.3%, and 5.7% respectively
context: Marijuana Business Daily conducted a survey of individuals with any ownership stake in a cannabis business and estimated that 81% of cannabis businesses are owned by those who identify as White. Asian, Black, and Hispanic/Latinx people make up 2.4%, 4.3%, and 5.7%, respectively, of cannabis business owners.
notes: Source: Marijuana Business Daily survey, Sept. 2017; may suffer from selection bias

claim: As of December 2020, Illinois had not issued a single social equity cannabis license and there was not one licensed cannabis business with a majority owner who is a person of color
context: Illinois was supposed to start issuing social equity licenses by April 30, 2020, but as of December 2020, a piece in the Chicago Tribune reported that "[n]ot one social equity license has been issued and there's still not one licensed cannabis business in the state with a majority owner who is a person of color."
notes: Source: Chicago Tribune, Jan. 4, 2021

claim: Massachusetts issued more than 70 cannabis licenses to social equity, economic empowerment, and Disadvantaged Business Enterprise applicants but only 3 had opened by July 16, 2020
context: Massachusetts has arguably the most aggressive social equity program in the country, but of the more than seventy licenses issued to social equity, economic empowerment, and Disadvantaged Business Enterprise applicants, only three had opened up as of July 16, 2020.
notes: Source: MassLive, July 16, 2020

claim: Massachusetts voted in May 2020 to allow cannabis delivery licenses to be exclusively issued for the next two years to social equity and economic empowerment applicants
context: Massachusetts's Cannabis Control Commission voted in May 2020 to allow licenses for delivery services to be exclusively issued for the next two years to social equity and economic empowerment applicants.

claim: There are an estimated 165,000–200,000 full-time equivalent workers supporting the cannabis industry in the US (2019 estimate from one industry source)
context: One industry source put the 2019 figure of workers "who support the marijuana industry" between 165,000-200,000 full-time equivalents ("FTE"), and another source estimated that legal cannabis supported 211,000 FTE jobs in 2019 and 243,700 jobs as of early 2020.
notes: Sources: Marijuana Business Daily (165k-200k) and Leafly Jobs Report 2020 (211k-243.7k)

claim: Legal cannabis supported 211,000 FTE jobs in 2019 and 243,700 jobs as of early 2020 per Leafly's Jobs Report
context: another source estimated that legal cannabis supported 211,000 FTE jobs in 2019 and 243,700 jobs as of early 2020.
notes: Source: Leafly Jobs Report 2020

claim: In Massachusetts, nearly 36% of approved/pending cannabis agents were female as of November 19, 2020
context: As of November 19, 2020, nearly 36% of the approved/pending agents were female.
notes: Source: Mass. Cannabis Control Commission Monthly Public Meeting, November 2020

claim: Massachusetts cannabis workforce racial breakdown: 6.6% Black/African American, 7.5% Hispanic/Latino, 80.5% White, 5.4% other/multiple races (November 2020)
context: Among those agents who answered the race/ethnicity question, 6.6% identified as Black/African American, 7.5% identified as Hispanic/Latino/Spanish, 80.5% identified as White, and 5.4% identified as another or multiple race/ethnicities.
notes: Compared to MA general population: 51.5% female, 9.0% Black, 12.4% Hispanic/Latinx (July 2019)

claim: Massachusetts general population as of July 2019: 51.5% female, 9.0% Black/African American, 12.4% Hispanic/Latinx
context: Those figures suggest that disparities persist when compared to the general population in Massachusetts where, as of July 2019, 51.5% are female, 9.0% are Black/African American, and 12.4% are Hispanic/Latinx.
notes: Source: U.S. Census Bureau QuickFacts Massachusetts

claim: Five years after licensed cannabis stores opened in Colorado and Washington, annual cannabis tax revenues were roughly $300 million and $400 million respectively
context: Five years after the licensed stores opened in Colorado and Washington, the annual cannabis tax revenues were roughly $300 million and $400 million, respectively. In both states, that works out to about $50 per resident and accounts for less than 1% of total state expenditures.
notes: Figures in millions; $50 per resident in both states; <1% of total state expenditures

claim: From 2014 to 2019, Colorado generated $1 billion in cannabis tax and fee revenues, with more than half spent on human services, public health and environment, and 17% on education
context: From 2014 to 2019, Colorado generated $1 billion in cannabis tax and fee revenues, with more than half of those funds being spent on human services, public health, and the environment, and another 17% on education.
notes: Source: Colorado Sun, June 12, 2019

claim: Washington generated $396 million in cannabis revenues for fiscal year 2019, with healthcare as the majority of expenditures
context: For the $396 million Washington generated in cannabis revenues for the fiscal year 2019, providing health care services accounted for the majority of expenditures.
notes: Washington Marijuana Revenues breakdown for FY 2019

claim: Washington State cannabis revenue FY 2019: General Fund $116.5M, Basic Health $188.3M, Cities/Counties $15.0M, Education/Prevention $9.5M, Research $0.4M, Other $49.2M
context:
• General Fund – $116.5 million
• Basic Health – $188.3 million
• Cities, Counties – $15.0 million
• Education, Prevention – $9.5 million
• Research – $0.4 million
• Other – $49.2 million

claim: Illinois R3 (Restore, Reinvest, Renew) program receives 25% of net adult-use cannabis tax revenue and was allocated $10 million in fiscal year 2020 for DAC grants
context: The R3 (Restore. Reinvest. Renew.) program in Illinois receives 25% of net tax revenue from adult-use cannabis sales and was allocated $10 million in fiscal year 2020 to disperse as grants investing in the revitalization of DACs.
notes: $31.5 million available for FY2021 grants per Jan. 21, 2021 press release

claim: Evanston, Illinois voted to use cannabis tax funds estimated at $500,000 per year for racial reparations for effects of slavery and drug prohibition, potentially the first such program in the country
context: Evanston, Illinois voted to use funds collected from cannabis taxation to fund racial reparations for the enduring effects of slavery and drug prohibition. Utilizing the first $10 million collected, this reparations program may be the first of its type in the country... City staff estimated Evanston will collect at least $500,000 a year from the tax, which can start being applied in July 2020.
notes: First $10M to be used for reparations; program tied to first $10M collected from cannabis tax

claim: The average potency of seized cannabis did not exceed 5% THC until the twenty-first century; today average flower potency in state-licensed stores is around 20% THC
context: The average potency of seized cannabis did not exceed 5% THC until the twenty-first century. Today, the average flower potency in state-licensed stores is around 20% THC, and—of perhaps even greater concern—the potency of extract-based products such as dabs can sometimes exceed 80% THC.
notes: Source: ElSohly et al. (2016), Biological Psychiatry; MPG Consulting & CU Boulder 2019 Market Update

claim: Extract-based cannabis products such as dabs can sometimes exceed 80% THC
context: the potency of extract-based products such as dabs can sometimes exceed 80% THC.
notes: Source: MPG Consulting & CU Boulder Leeds School of Business, 2019 Regulated Marijuana Market Update

claim: A 2020 working group concluded that THC content contributes to adverse health effects in a dose-response manner, with higher risks particularly for young users and those with pre-existing mental health conditions, likely disproportionately affecting marginalized populations
context: research available to date documents that THC content of cannabis products contributes to adverse health effects in a dose-response manner. This increased risk imposed from using higher potency cannabis products is particularly concerning for young users and those with certain pre-existing mental health conditions. These harms are likely to disproportionately affect marginalized populations (low income, minorities) who choose high potency products because of their lower costs, ease and discrete nature of use, glamorization of its use through social media and advertising, and perception of safety.
notes: Source: Joint UW & WSU Workgroup, Cannabis Concentration and Health Risks Report, Nov. 2020

claim: Colorado circa 2015: marijuana stores were more likely to locate in neighborhoods with a higher proportion of racial and ethnic minority population, lower household income, higher crime rate, or greater density of on-premise alcohol outlets
context: An analysis of the location of medical and recreational cannabis stores in Colorado circa 2015 found that "marijuana stores were more likely to locate in neighborhoods that had a lower proportion of young people, had a higher proportion of racial and ethnic minority population, had a lower household income, had a higher crime rate, or had a greater density of on-premise alcohol outlets."
notes: Source: Shi, Meseck & Jankowska (2016), Journal of Addiction

claim: A California study of 1,100+ cannabis retailers in October 2018 found neighborhoods with retailers had higher proportions of Hispanics, African Americans, and residents living below the poverty level
context: A similar study of more than 1,100 cannabis retailers—licensed and unlicensed—in California in October 2018 found that "[r]elative to neighborhoods without retailers, neighborhoods with retailers had higher proportions of Hispanics, African Americans, and residents living below the poverty level."
notes: Source: Unger et al. (2020), Preventive Medicine Reports

claim: Washington State 2014-2017: density of all licensed cannabis outlets increased over time; most-deprived areas had increased likelihood of licensed cannabis outlet density compared to least-deprived areas
context: The density of all licensed cannabis outlets increased over time. Most‐deprived areas have an increased likelihood of licensed cannabis outlet density when compared to least‐deprived areas. No differences were observed in the likelihood of licensed cannabis outlet density in middle‐deprived areas when compared to least‐deprived areas.
notes: Source: Amiri et al. (2019), Drug & Alcohol Review; analyzed using Area Deprivation Index

claim: A Portland, Oregon study of 117 neighborhoods found cannabis retailers more likely to be located in relatively deprived neighborhoods
context: A similar study focused on 117 neighborhoods in Portland, Oregon, found evidence that "cannabis retailers are more likely to be located in relatively deprived neighborhoods, suggesting the need to consider spatial equity in cannabis policies to mitigate disproportionate exposure to retailers, particularly if retailer exposure is associated with negative consequences."
notes: Source: Firth et al. (2020), Cannabis journal

claim: Adults living within 0.8 miles of a Washington State cannabis retailer had significantly higher odds of past-month use (OR=1.45) and frequent use on 20+ days per month (OR=1.43)
context: past-month use of cannabis "increased among adults living in areas within 18 miles of a retailer and, especially, within 0.8 miles (odds ratio [OR] = 1.45; 95% confidence interval [CI] = 1.24, 1.69). Frequent use [use on at least 20 of the past 30 days] increased among adults living within 0.8 miles of a retailer (OR = 1.43; 95% CI = 1.15, 1.77)."
notes: Source: Everson et al. (2019), Am. J. Public Health

claim: From 2010 to 2019, there were approximately 150,000 arrests and 120,000 convictions for cannabis possession in Virginia, with about 50,000 of the 90,000 adult convictions being BIPOC
context: from 2010 to 2019, there were about 150,000 arrests and 120,000 convictions for (misdemeanor) cannabis possession in Virginia, with about three-fourths of those being of individuals twenty-one years or older and, hence, pertaining to behavior that would not be prohibited after legalization. Of those 90,000 adult convictions for simple possession, just over 50,000 were BIPOC.
notes: Data from Virginia JLARC; misdemeanor possession only

claim: Automatically expunging all past cannabis possession records in Virginia would benefit more than 100,000 BIPOC and more than 100,000 others
context: it is safe to say that automatically expunging all past criminal records for adult cannabis possession offenses in Virginia would benefit more than 100,000 BIPOC and more than 100,000 others as well.
notes: Conservative estimate based on 2010-2019 data; actual number over 40 years much higher

claim: There are approximately 20 cannabis jobs per $1 million in sales, or one job per $50,000 in annual sales
context: Passing a straight line through a scatterplot of their state-specific job estimates versus annual sales shows that, in round numbers, there are twenty jobs per $1 million in sales, which is one job for every $50,000 in sales.
notes: Based on Leafly employment and revenue data analysis; regression R²=0.997

claim: Colorado had $1.75 billion in combined medical and retail cannabis sales in 2019 and reported 34,705 total cannabis employees, yielding one job per $50,400 in sales
context: Colorado had $1.75 billion in sales in 2019 (combined medical and retail) and reports total employment as 34,705, or one job for every $50,400 in sales.
notes: Source: MPG Consulting & CU Boulder Leeds School of Business, 2019 Regulated Marijuana Market Update

claim: Statistics Canada reported federally licensed medical cannabis producers had $647 million CAD in sales and 9,200 employees, approximately one job per $70,000 CAD (close to $50,000 USD at 2020 exchange rates)
context: Statistics Canada reports that federally licensed medical cannabis producers had $647 million in sales and 9,200 employees, or one employee for every $70,000 Canadian in sales, which is close to one job per $50,000 U.S. at 2020 exchange rates.
notes: Source: Statistics Canada, Aug. 2019; CAD figures

claim: Virginia's cannabis market might reach roughly $500 million per year after legalization, supporting approximately 10,000 cannabis jobs in the short to medium term
context: With the Virginia legislature recently passing a bill to legalize the production and commercial sales of cannabis, its cannabis market might reach roughly $500 million per year a few years after legalization is implemented. Using that $50,000 in sales per job estimate, that suggests that the cannabis industry might employ roughly 10,000 people in the short to medium term.
notes: Based on scaling Massachusetts and Washington estimates by population and prevalence

claim: Virginia may have approximately 1,000 licensed cannabis locations and several hundred cannabis businesses, with perhaps several dozen owned by DAC entrepreneurs
context: one might estimate that the number of licensed cannabis locations in a state the size of Virginia will be on the order of 1,000 (all licensed premises, including producers and distributors, not just retail). At first, the number of businesses may be perhaps half that amount... it is perhaps reasonable to expect that there may be several hundred cannabis companies based in a state the size of Virginia, thus several hundred local entrepreneurs.
notes: Massachusetts used as comparator with 835 pending/approved applications including 326 retail as of July 2020

claim: Massachusetts had 835 pending or approved cannabis license applications as of July 2020, including 326 for retail; 20% qualified as a disadvantaged business enterprise
context: Massachusetts's Cannabis Control Commission reports that as of July 2020, it had 835 pending or approved license applications, including 326 for retail. Of those, 20% qualified as a disadvantaged business enterprise for being women owned, veteran owned, minority owned, disability owned, and/or LGBT owned, although many of those have not yet opened.
notes: Source: Massachusetts Cannabis Control Commission July 2020 Public Meeting Packet

claim: Cannabis wholesale production costs have fallen from $2,000–$6,000 per pound in California in 2010 to below $1 CAD per gram (about $300 USD per pound) for some Canadian producers by November 2019
context: The wholesale price of high-potency cannabis "in California in 2010 was $2,000 - $6,000 per pound . . . , and generally higher in other parts of the U.S." Given inflation, that is equivalent to $2,325-$6,975 per pound in 2019 dollars. By November 2019, a number of Canadian producers boasted of production costs below $1 Canadian per gram, which is equivalent to about $300 U.S. per pound.
notes: Source: Caulkins, 'Radical Technological Breakthroughs in Drugs and Drug Markets'

claim: Before the 2009 Ogden memo, many grow operations stayed under 99 plants; average size of cannabis firms in one study was under 1,000 square feet; by comparison, some Canadian producers now have 1 million square foot grow operations
context: Before the 2009 Ogden memo, many grow operations kept to ninety-nine plants to stay under the 100-plant threshold that triggered longer sentences. The average size of the 186 firms in Angela Hawken and James Prieger's study sample was just under 1,000 square feet (about double the size of a typical two-car garage). A sample of ten gray-market firms analyzed in 2013 had an average facility size of 3,000 square feet of grow area. Now, a number of Canadian producers have 1 million square foot grow ops (about five times the size of a typical Walmart Superstore).
notes: Source: Hawken & Prieger (2013), BOTEC Analysis; Caulkins, 'Radical Technological Breakthroughs'

claim: All cannabis consumed in the US could theoretically be produced on approximately 10 average-sized farms (444 acres each) based on 2016 consumption estimates of 7,700 metric tons
context: The most recent national estimate of cannabis consumption is from 2016: roughly 7,700 metric ton equivalents of flower, or 7.7 billion grams. Assuming an outdoor yield of forty grams per square foot per harvest—and only one harvest per year—one would need 192,500,000 square feet, or 4,419 acres... this suggests that one could produce all of the cannabis consumed in the United States on roughly ten average-sized farms.
notes: Source: RAND, What America's Users Spend on Illegal Drugs 2006-2016; USDA Farm & Land in Farms 2019; even at 15,000 metric tons at 30g/sqft would only need 26 average farms

claim: Ohio's medical cannabis law (2016) required at least 15% of cannabis licenses be issued to economically disadvantaged groups including Blacks, American Indians, Hispanics/Latinos, and Asians, but was struck down by Ohio courts as unconstitutional
context: Ohio's medical cannabis law, passed in 2016, required at least 15% of cultivator, processor, laboratory, and retailer licenses be issued to "entities that are owned and controlled by United States citizens who are residents of [Ohio] and are members of one of the following economically disadvantaged groups: Blacks or African Americans, American Indians, Hispanics or Latinos, and Asians." A non-minority-owned company seeking a cannabis production license sued the Ohio Department of Commerce... The judge ruled in favor of the company, ordering that the provision be severed and stricken from the law.
notes: Case: Pharmacann Ohio, LLC v. Williams, No. 17-CV-010962, 2018 WL 7500067 (Ohio Ct. Com. Pl. Nov. 15, 2018)

claim: Roughly 100,000 Americans die from alcohol-related causes each year
context: it is often argued that cannabis is less dangerous than alcohol, but that is not saying much since roughly 100,000 Americans die from alcohol-related causes each year.
notes: Source: Esser et al. (2020), MMWR; CDC Alcohol-Related Disease Impact (ARDI)

claim: Most cannabis sales are to daily and near-daily users; daily/near-daily users account for most of cannabis consumption
context: most sales are to daily and near-daily users. The social equity concern is that a for-profit legal cannabis industry will concentrate marketing and sales in vulnerable populations, as the alcohol and tobacco industries have done.
notes: Source: Kilmer et al., RAND, What America's Users Spend on Illegal Drugs: 2000-2010 (2014)

claim: About 40% of cannabis stores in Alberta, Canada are owned by just eight companies despite regulations preventing any one entity from holding more than 15% of retail cannabis licenses
context: About 40% of stores in Alberta, Canada are owned by just eight companies—even though regulations prevent any one person or entity from holding more than 15% of retail cannabis licenses in the province.
notes: Source: Alberta Gaming, Liquor & Cannabis Commission

claim: Retail cannabis stores charge about triple the wholesale price (e.g., $9 per gram retail for cannabis that wholesales for $3 per gram), while Amazon can deliver a package for $2–$8
context: It only costs Amazon between $2-$8 to deliver a package, but brick-and-mortar retail cannabis stores now charge about triple the wholesale price—say $9 per gram for cannabis that wholesales for $3 per gram.

claim: Virginia and Massachusetts cannabis market comparison: MA past-month use rate 14.9% vs VA 7.9%, MA population about 20% lower than VA
context: Massachusetts may be a relevant comparator; its population is about 20% lower than that of Virginia, but its past-month marijuana use rates are almost twice as high (14.9% versus 7.9% among adults).
notes: Source: SAMHSA 2018-2019 National Survey on Drug Use and Health

claim: The Denver cannabis industry 2020 convenience sample: 68% of employees identified as White, 12% Hispanic/Latino, 6% Black (but 26%+ did not disclose race/ethnicity)
context: a report based on a convenience sample of those working in the Denver cannabis industry in 2020 estimated that 68% percent of employees identified as White, 12% as Hispanic/Latino/Spanish, and 6% as Black; however, the authors urged caution when interpreting these figures because over 26% of study respondents chose not to disclose their race/ethnicity.
notes: Source: AnalyticInsight, Cannabis Business and Employment Opportunity Study (2020); convenience sample, caution advised

claim: A case study concludes that record clearing intervention helps: employment rates and earnings appear to rise during or immediately after expungement, though the staying power is still unclear
context: the record clearing intervention delivers on its promise. People with criminal records seek the unmarking remedy after a period of declining earnings, in spite of active labor market participation. During or immediately after the intervention, average employment rates and earnings appear to rise, though the staying power of such increases is still unclear.
notes: Source: Selbin, McCrary & Epstein (2018), J. Crim. L. & Criminology; also Prescott & Starr (2020), Harvard Law Review

claim: By expunging past cannabis records a state like Virginia could benefit hundreds of thousands; employment in cannabis could benefit thousands; equity business ownership could directly help several dozen
context: By expunging past records of cannabis law violations, a state the size of Virginia could improve the employment prospects of hundreds of thousands of people. Increasing employment of DAC members in the cannabis industry could benefit thousands. Equity programs directed at the owners of cannabis businesses could directly help several dozen. All are helpful, and all can be done simultaneously. But the scale in terms of numbers of direct beneficiaries is sharply different.
notes: This is the paper's central quantitative finding about relative scale of equity programs

claim: One study found after legalization there was an increase in the number of people aged 12-17 and 26 and older meeting clinical criteria for cannabis use disorder
context: one study found that after legalization there was an increase in the number of people aged 12-17 and 26 and older meeting clinical criteria for cannabis use disorder.
notes: Source: Cerdá et al. (2020), JAMA Psychiatry; Association Between Recreational Marijuana Legalization and Changes in Marijuana Use and CUD 2008-2016

claim: A state-store model for cannabis distribution would likely generate more government revenue and fewer health harms in DACs than a profit-maximizing commercial model
context: A state monopoly over retail distribution would offer a jurisdiction the greatest potential cannabis-based revenue that could be used to fund efforts to reduce social inequities... The government monopoly approach is also expected to be less likely to exacerbate health inequities compared to the profit-maximization model. A state monopoly over retail distribution with appropriate restrictions would offer better opportunities to decrease inequities and ensure that harms from regulations and legislative programs are not disproportionate to harms of the substance itself.
notes: Rhode Island, Pennsylvania governors proposed state-store model; Virginia JLARC discussed it in 2020

claim: Washington State legal cannabis market had trended towards higher-THC products, with flower above 20% THC and extracts above 60% THC becoming commonplace, based on 30 million cannabis sales
context: Washington's legal cannabis market has trended towards higher-THC products, as flower products with THC concentration more than 20% and extract products with more than 60% THC are now commonplace.
notes: Source: Smart, Caulkins, Kilmer et al. (2017), Addiction; analysis of 30 million cannabis sales

claim: BIPOC-owned businesses are more likely to hire BIPOC employees: 93%+ of Black business employers rely on minorities for 50%+ of jobs, while 60% of nonminority employers have no minority employees
context: While over 93 percent of black business employers rely upon minorities to fill 50 percent or more of their available jobs, nearly 60 percent of the nonminority employers have no minority employees.
notes: Source: Bates (1994), Review of Black Political Economy; supports rationale for BIPOC license preferences

claim: Non-White people made up only 16% of leaders of licensed cannabis producers and parent companies in Canada's legal cannabis industry
context: non-White people made up 16% of leaders of licensed cannabis producers and parent companies.
notes: Source: Centre on Drug Policy Evaluation, How Diverse Is Canada's Legal Cannabis Industry? (2020)

claim: 20% of all employer businesses in the US were at least 51% owned by minority entrepreneurs
context: "20 percent of all employer businesses were at least 51 percent owned by minority entrepreneurs."
notes: Source: U.S. Small Business Administration, Small Business Facts: Spotlight on Minority-Owned Employer Businesses (2019)

claim: The R3 program in Illinois had $31.5 million available for grants in fiscal year 2021
context: Pritzker Admin. Awards $31.5 Million in First Ever Restore, Reinvest & Renew Program Grants to Orgs Across the State (Jan. 21, 2021)... noting that $31.5 million is available for grants for fiscal year 2021.
notes: Source: Illinois Criminal Justice Information Authority press release, Jan. 21, 2021

claim: Cannabis was in use as early as 2700 BC as a textile and for religious, recreational, and medicinal purposes in diverse societies around the globe
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: As early as 2700 BC, cannabis was in use as a textile as well as religiously, recreationally, and medicinally in diverse societies around the globe, including Western, Southern, and Eastern Asia and in indigenous and Latin American cultures in North, Central, and South America.
notes: PNAS Nexus, 2023, 2, 1-7; doi 10.1093/pnasnexus/pgad383; published Dec. 12, 2023

claim: Marijuana was listed as a Schedule I drug in 1971, and since that time millions of Americans have been prosecuted for marijuana-related offenses with striking racial disparities in arrest rates
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: Circumstances reached a fever pitch in 1971 when marijuana was listed as a Schedule I drug. Since that time, millions of Americans have been prosecuted for marijuana-related offenses with striking racial disparities in arrest rates.

claim: Even though recreational cannabis use is legal in 37 states and DC, there were still over 170,000 marijuana possession-related arrests in the United States in 2021 that disproportionately targeted communities of color
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: Now, even though recreational cannabis use is legal in 37 states and the District of Columbia, there were still over 170,000 marijuana possession-related arrests in the United States in 2021 that continued to disproportionately target communities of color.
notes: Source: ACLU, A Tale of Two Countries (2020)

claim: Federal simple marijuana possession conviction can result in fines between $1,000 and $5,000 and incarceration of 1-3 years, and adds criminal history points that affect future sentencing
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: Offenders receive criminal "history" points, can be fined between $1,000 and $5,000, and can be incarcerated for between one and three years.
notes: Source: US Sentencing Commission, Weighing the Impact of Simple Possession of Marijuana (2023)

claim: Cannabis use rates between Hispanic, Black, and White groups are comparable in epidemiological data, yet communities of color have been disproportionately prosecuted and incarcerated
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: there is evidence that communities of color have been disproportionately prosecuted and incarcerated, even though epidemiological data suggest cannabis use rates between Hispanic, black, and white groups are comparable.
notes: Source: Keyes et al. (2017), Drug and Alcohol Dependence; Race/ethnicity and marijuana use in the US: diminishing differences 2006-2015

claim: In Washington State it took eight years to expunge records of those convicted of cannabis charges before legalization; in contrast, California made expungement part of state legalization in 2016
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: in the state of Washington it took eight years to expunge the records of those convicted of cannabis charges before legalization, while in contrast, California made expungement a part of state legalization in 2016.
notes: Illustrates wide variation in expungement approaches across states

claim: Barriers preventing minority cannabis business ownership include high application fees and deposits, inability to secure cannabis business loans, and rules preventing those with prior convictions from starting businesses
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: Barriers including high application fees and deposits on licenses, the inability to secure cannabis-related business loans, and rules preventing individuals with previous convictions from starting cannabis businesses mean that the economic benefits of the so-called "Green Rush" are inequitably distributed, and a great deal of cannabis business has been concentrated among a small group: namely, wealthy white men.

claim: The CEOs of the three cannabis companies with the greatest market share in the US (Northwest Cannabis Solutions, Copperstate Farms LLC, and Mindful Medical) are all white, and over 81% of all cannabis business leadership is white
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: For instance, the CEOs of the three cannabis companies with the greatest market share in the United States (Northwest Cannabis Solutions, Copperstate Farms LLC, and Mindful Medical) are all white, with a recent analysis reporting that over 81% of all cannabis business leadership is white.
notes: Source: MJBizDaily (2017)

claim: Cannabis-based pharmaceuticals are overwhelmingly produced by white, male-led corporations including Greenwich Biosciences (Epidiolex), Abbott Products (Marinol), GW Pharmaceuticals (Sativex), and Sanofi-Aventis (Acomplia)
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: cannabis-based pharmaceuticals are overwhelmingly produced by white, male-led corporations, including Greenwich Biosciences (the producers of Epidiolex), Abbott Products (Marinol), GW Pharmaceuticals (Sativex), and Sanofi-Aventis (Acomplia).
notes: Notable exception: Solvay Pharmaceuticals, producers of Syndros

claim: New York reserved a proportion of dispensary licenses for those directly affected by a marijuana-related incarceration; Colorado created an 'accelerator program' to increase cannabis licenses for people of color
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: New York has reserved a proportion of dispensary licenses for those directly affected by a marijuana-related incarceration, while Colorado's "accelerator program" is designed to help increase the number of cannabis business licenses for people of color.
notes: Massachusetts Cannabis Social Equity Fund also mentioned: provides grants and loans to people of color (Mass. General Laws c.94G § 14A)

claim: Legalization has been largely motivated by economic forces while research on risks or benefits of cannabis—hampered by federal laws—is trailing behind usage trends and not informing policy as it should
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: legalization has been largely motivated by economic forces while research on the risks or benefits of cannabis—hampered in large part by federal laws and regulations—is trailing largely behind usage trends and thus not informing policy as it should.

claim: Many health care providers do not feel confident advising the public on risks or benefits of cannabis and often have a poor understanding of their state's cannabis laws
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: many health care providers do not feel confident advising the public on the risks or benefits of cannabis, and often have a poor understanding of their state's cannabis laws.
notes: Sources: Carliner et al. (2017), Carlini et al. (2017), Ricco et al. (2017)

claim: Biomedical research has been conducted predominantly with white males of high socioeconomic status, and cannabis research is no exception
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: it is also well documented that biomedical research has been conducted predominantly with participants who are white males of high socioeconomic status (e.g. 58), and cannabis research is no exception.
notes: Source: Robinson, McMichael & Hernandez (2017), JAMA Dermatology; Bidwell, Martin-Willett & Karoly (2021)

claim: There are currently no statistics reporting on the demographics of cannabis-focused scientists in academia or industry, making it impossible to assess diversity in the cannabis research workforce
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: there are currently no statistics reporting on the demographics of cannabis-focused scientists in academia or in industry. Though sorely needed, this work would be challenging, given how interdisciplinary cannabis science is inclusive of fields like plant biology, genetics, agriculture, chemistry and biochemistry, medicine, and psychology and psychiatry to name a few.

claim: The 1967 President's Commission on Crime recognized that US drug policies were discriminatory and ineffective
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: Though it had been recognized by the President's Commission on Crime in 1967 that US drug policies were discriminatory and ineffective, legislators in the 1960s grew increasingly preoccupied with cannabis regulation, perhaps linked to the fact that young white adults in the middle and upper classes were using cannabis in large numbers.
notes: Source: King (1994), Police Journal; analysis of 1967 commission's impact

claim: The Marijuana Tax Act of 1937 did not criminalize cannabis outright but imposed exorbitantly high 'taxes' on anyone found growing, selling, or possessing cannabis; federal non-medical ban came five years after Prohibition ended
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: cannabis was not banned from nonmedical use federally until five years after Prohibition ended. Of note, the Marijuana Tax Act of 1937 did not criminalize cannabis outright but imposed exorbitantly high "taxes" on anyone found growing, selling, or possessing cannabis.
notes: Source: Bender (2016), UC Davis Law Review; Moran (2010)

claim: White legislators in Western and Southeastern US used racialized language to argue for cannabis prohibition despite the fact that few in the Hispanic community used cannabis; actual motivation was rising use among young white people
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: white legislators in the Western and Southeastern United States used racialized language in their arguments for cannabis prohibition that were in reality prompted by fears surrounding rising use by young white people.
notes: Sources: Bender (2016); Campos (2013); Fisher (2021)

claim: A history of incarceration is significantly related to recidivism and future mental and physical health problems; incarceration creates or intensifies financial hardships and barriers to employment, business loans, certain industries, and federal benefits
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: A history of incarceration is significantly related to recidivism and future mental and physical health problems. Incarceration can create or intensify financial hardships, affect one's ability to gain employment, secure a business or personal loan, work in certain industries, become politically involved, gain or keep a green card, and receive federal benefits such as student loans.
notes: Sources: Harding et al. (2017), PNAS; Schnittker et al. (2012); Massoglia & Remster (2019); Harper et al. (2021)

claim: Having an incarcerated parent is associated with long-term financial hardship, lower graduation rates, and poorer mental and physical health for children
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: having an incarcerated parent is associated with long long-term financial hardship, lower graduation rates, and poorer mental and physical health for children.
notes: Sources: Hairston (2007), Annie E. Casey Foundation; Shaw (2022), Family Relations

claim: Post-legalization cannabis policies are being developed without the input of minority stakeholders and cannabis remains federally illegal as a Schedule I substance
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: Disappointingly, post legalization cannabis policies are being developed without the input of minority stakeholders or, to a large extent, the research community. This lack of input in combination with the fact that cannabis remains federally illegal and classified as a Schedule I substance has led to continued inequality.
notes: Sources: Danquah-Brobby (2017), Univ. Baltimore Law Rev.; Schlussel (2017), California Law Rev.

claim: Communities of color continue to be excluded from most legal state cannabis markets, and many efforts proceed without increased key stakeholder input from people of color
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: Despite these efforts, however, communities of color continue to be excluded from most legal state markets, and many efforts continue to proceed without increased key stakeholder input from people of color. Given the fact that communities of color have been the most impacted by inequitable drug policy and enforcement historically, frustration and resentment continues to grow.
notes: Sources include multiple news/opinion articles 2016-2021

claim: Research on cannabis health effects is hampered because researchers cannot study legal market products on campuses and must rely on NIDA-approved suppliers with limited formulations not representative of legal market products
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: researchers cannot study legal market products on campuses and must rely on NIDA-approved suppliers with limited formulations that are not representative of the products available on the legal market. Additionally, it remains challenging for cannabis researchers to obtain permits from the FDA to study different cannabis formulations.
notes: Source: Vergara et al. (2017), Scientific Reports: federally produced cannabis does not reflect legal markets

claim: If cannabis were reclassified from Schedule I, banks could access FDIC deposit insurance for cannabis funds, businesses could receive federal bankruptcy protection and apply for federal tax deductions, potentially opening the industry to more small businesses led by people of color
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: if cannabis were no longer a Schedule I drug, banks trying to conduct business within the cannabis industry would no longer be exposed to the risk of criminal charges or regulatory violations and could access FDIC deposit insurance for cannabis business funds. Once the cannabis industry shifts away from a cash-only business model, they would not rank as highly in terms of risk for insurers, and they would also be eligible to receive federal bankruptcy protection and apply federal tax deductions. These changes could then potentially open the industry to more small businesses, especially those led by people of color.
notes: Sources: Ward et al. (2019), Bellano (2022), Kavousi et al. (2022)

claim: California's Community Reinvestment Grants (CalCRG) program is cited as an example of state-run public funding for cannabis equity that could distribute more profit from the industry to communities most impacted by criminalization
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: state-run public funding schemes could also follow this pattern (e.g. California's Community Reinvestment Grants (CalCRG) program).
notes: Source: Jaeger (2020), California Governor's Office of Business and Economic Development

claim: Cannabis policy and research face five key action areas: equity-focused legislation/policy, supporting entry/retention of scientists of color, ethical research practices, and inclusive recruitment of participants
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: we propose five key actions in both policy and research domains that are necessary to move the field of cannabis research, and perhaps biomedical research in substance use more broadly, forward in a productive and inclusionary way. Specifically, recommendations focus on equity-focused legislation and policy, supporting the entry and retention of scientists of color into the field, engaging in more ethical research practices, and practicing intentionally inclusive recruitment of participants.

table_data.action_2: Supporting entry and retention of scientists of color
table_data.action_5: Supporting underrepresented groups in cannabis industry

notes: Table 1 in the paper summarizes these suggested actionable steps

claim: Substance use laws have been an arm of institutional power for hundreds of years and are often selectively enacted or enforced against minoritized communities, contributing to inequity in both the legal cannabis industry and cannabis research
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: Substance use laws have been an arm of institutional power for hundreds of years and are often selectively enacted or enforced against minoritized communities. This has led to generations of people of color being disproportionately prosecuted and incarcerated for cannabis-related infractions, which in turn has contributed to inequity in the legal-market cannabis industry and in cannabis research.
notes: Conclusion of the paper

claim: The cannabis industry's Schedule I classification limits banking access, decreases insurance coverage options, and creates tax difficulties under the specter of prosecution risk
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: addressing the current classification of cannabis under federal law as a Schedule I drug, which limits banking access, decreases insurance coverage options, and creates tax difficulties all under the specter of the risk of prosecution.

claim: Stigma and fear associated with medical cannabis use are barriers to research participation, with willingness to participate closely linked to levels of perceived stigma
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: Some recent work suggests that there is stigma and fear associated with medical cannabis use and worry about interactions with medical providers and law enforcement. We were able to find one small study of stigma and cannabis research participation in general (not accounting for the race or ethnicity of respondents), that suggested willingness to participate in research was closely linked to levels of perceived stigma.
notes: Source: Hohl (2018), Medical Marijuana and Stigma; doctoral dissertation, University of the Rockies

claim: STEM fields have problems recruiting, supporting, and retaining scientists from underrepresented groups, and even when retained there are substantial racial and gender pay gaps
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: STEM fields in general have problems recruiting, supporting, and retaining scientists from underrepresented groups; and even when these scientists are retained in research, there are substantial racial and gender pay gaps.
notes: Source: Fry, Kennedy & Funk (2021), Pew Research: STEM jobs see uneven progress in increasing gender, racial and ethnic diversity

claim: The field of cannabis research would benefit from concentrated efforts to improve STEM outcomes for underrepresented minority individuals, including collecting demographic data on degree completion, restructuring courses, and increasing financial support
source_report: Scholarly/Effect of inequity and institutional power.pdf
context: The field would benefit from more research on the demographics of cannabis science, specifically, but also from concentrated efforts to improve STEM outcomes for underrepresented minority individuals. Some of the recommendations in the literature include requiring universities to collect demographic data on degree completion and switching/dropping rates among students, restructuring courses to be more welcoming to novices and inclusive of diverse students, increasing financial support and resources for STEM students from minoritized groups.
notes: Sources: Estrada et al. (2016), Kendricks et al. (2019)

claim: Approximately 21-30% of cannabis sales in Nevada occur through unlicensed transactions
context: Eliminating the Unlicensed Market: Nevada's unlicensed cannabis market is multi-faceted and includes both large-scale traffickers as well as smaller operators and individuals. Approximately 21-30% of cannabis sales in Nevada occur through unlicensed transactions, posing safety issues for the public and tourists and harming the state's efforts to create a healthy, controlled marketplace.
notes: Range of 21-30% cited; sourced from a 2024 report commissioned by the State of Nevada (TPMA)

claim: Nevada's unlicensed cannabis market represents approximately $242 million to $370 million in sales upon which state tax is not being collected
context: Within Nevada, a considerable segment of cannabis sales occurs outside the regulated framework. A 2024 report, commissioned by the State of Nevada, estimates that 21-30% of cannabis sales are through unlicensed transactions—approximately $242 million to $370 million in sales upon which state tax is not being collected.
notes: Range estimate; sourced from Nevada Cannabis Compliance Board Cannabis Market Study prepared by TPMA (Aug. 2024)

claim: Nevada has the second largest illegal cannabis market per capita after California when comparing four western states
context: In addition, Nevada has the second largest illegal market per capita, after California, when comparing four western states.
notes: Comparison limited to four western states; sourced from TPMA Cannabis Market Study

claim: Washington State reports 77% of consumers obtain their cannabis from a licensed store, higher than the 57% average among all legal states
context: No state or jurisdiction has successfully eliminated unlicensed sources for cannabis, but many are having success speeding the transition and reducing public impacts. For example, Washington State reports 77% of consumers obtain their cannabis from a licensed store, which is higher than the 57% average among all legal states.
notes: Sourced from International Cannabis Policy Study, University of Waterloo (Sept. 2022)

claim: Average among all legal states for consumers obtaining cannabis from a licensed store is 57%
context: No state or jurisdiction has successfully eliminated unlicensed sources for cannabis, but many are having success speeding the transition and reducing public impacts. For example, Washington State reports 77% of consumers obtain their cannabis from a licensed store, which is higher than the 57% average among all legal states.
notes: Sourced from International Cannabis Policy Study, University of Waterloo (Sept. 2022)

claim: Canada reports as many as 70% of consumers are purchasing cannabis from legal sources
context: Canada has similarly reported as many as 70% of consumers are purchasing from legal sources with observable declines in illegal cannabis storefronts across its municipalities.
notes: Canada data, included for comparison; sourced from Health Canada (Oct. 2022)

claim: Las Vegas metropolitan area is visited by more than 40 million tourists each year
context: The Las Vegas metropolitan area is visited by more than 40 million tourists each year, most of whom are unaware of the state's cannabis purchase and consumption laws.
notes: Sourced from Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority 2023 Visitor Profile Study

claim: 70% of overnight guests in Las Vegas stay in the Strip Resort Corridor where legal cannabis operators are restricted from operating or delivering
context: Legal operators are restricted from operating in or delivering to the Strip Resort Corridor, where 70% of overnight guests stay.
notes: Sourced from Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority 2023 Visitor Profile Study

claim: Licensed cannabis stores in Nevada must be set back 1,500 feet from gaming establishments
context: Nevada legalized cannabis for adult-use in 2017, but restrictions in state law limit tourist access to regulated cannabis. Licensed stores must be set back 1,500 feet from gaming establishments, preventing any from opening on the Las Vegas Strip.
notes: Codified in NRS 678B.210(3)(a)(2)(II) and NRS 678B.250(3)(a)(2)(II)

claim: At least four murders associated with unlicensed cannabis sales occurred in Las Vegas over the last year
context: The unlicensed cannabis market is a public safety issue. Unlicensed sale of drugs, including cannabis, can lead to violence, resulting in injury or death. At least four murders associated with unlicensed sales have occurred in Las Vegas over the last year alone.
notes: Based on multiple media reports cited in footnotes 31-34

claim: Nevada derives 43% of its gross domestic product from tourism
context: For a state that derives 43% of its gross domestic product from tourism, safety risks for tourists could pose risks to the state's economy should a tourist be seriously hurt or killed.
notes: Sourced from Nevada Resort Association (Apr. 2023)

claim: Cannabis businesses pay an effective federal tax rate of up to 70% due to IRS Code 280E
context: IRC section 280E prohibits businesses that produce, transport or sell Schedule I drugs from deducting standard business expenses on their taxes. There are no exceptions for cannabis businesses legalized under state law. Because they cannot take standard business deductions, cannabis businesses pay an effective federal tax rate of up 70 percent. This creates a tremendous financial burden, particularly for small and equity operators.

claim: As of October 2024, 38 states allow medical cannabis and 24 states plus two US territories and DC have legalized recreational use
context: As of October 2024, 38 states allow medical cannabis and 24 states, two U.S. territories, and the District of Columbia have legalized recreational use.
notes: Sourced from National Conference of State Legislatures (June 2024)

claim: Public opinion on cannabis legalization shifted from 31% support in 2000 to 88% support for medical or recreational use in 2024
context: 2000: 31% believed that cannabis should be legalized. 2024: 88% now believe it should be legal for medical or recreational use.
notes: Sourced from Pew Research Center (Mar. 2024)

claim: 22% of Americans have chosen a travel destination based on whether adult-use cannabis is allowed
context: A 2022 Harris Poll found that: 22% of Americans have chosen a travel destination based whether adult-use cannabis is allowed.
notes: Sourced from 2022 Harris Poll

claim: 30% of American adults 21+ consider legal status of adult-use cannabis an important factor when choosing a vacation destination; 50% of millennials within this group
context: 30% of American adults 21 and older consider legal status of adult-use cannabis an important factor when choosing a vacation destination. Within this group, 50% of millennials consider cannabis an important factor.
notes: Sourced from 2022 Harris Poll

claim: More than two-thirds of people who use cannabis report cutting back on drinking
context: More than two-thirds of people who use cannabis report cutting back on drinking. More Americans now use cannabis on a daily or near daily frequency than alcohol.
notes: Sourced from TD Cowen report 'Cannabis Beats Booze' (Jan. 2024)

claim: More Americans now use cannabis on a daily or near daily frequency than alcohol
context: More Americans now use cannabis on a daily or near daily frequency than alcohol.
notes: Sourced from Caulkins (2024), Addiction journal, covering self-reported data from 1979-2022

claim: Nevada charges a Retail Cannabis Tax of 10% and a Wholesale Cannabis Tax of 15%
context: Nevada currently charges two cannabis-specific taxes: A Retail Cannabis Tax of 10% of the sale price, charged at the time of sale. A Wholesale Cannabis Tax of 15% of the fair market value, charged at the first wholesale sale.

claim: 50% of states that have legalized cannabis have adopted measures allocating funding for research
context: Some states have invested in cannabis research both to inform policy development and in response to this funding need. Fifty percent of states that have legalized cannabis have adopted measures allocating funding for research.
notes: Sourced from Balla, Boyle, and Dempsey (Aug. 2024) manuscript

claim: Arizona allocates up to $25 million in clinical trial cannabis research grants distributed over five years
context: Arizona: Up to $25 million in clinical trial research grants distributed over five years.
notes: From Arizona Proposition 207 (2020)

claim: Illinois allocates 2% of cannabis tax revenues for research-related activities including a Cannabis Research Institute
context: Illinois: 2% of cannabis tax revenues for research-related activities, including to fund a Cannabis Research Institute.
notes: From Illinois Cannabis Regulation and Tax Act (2019)

claim: Minnesota allocates $2.5 million annually to the University of Minnesota to fund a Center for Cannabis Research
context: Minnesota: $2.5 million annually to the University of Minnesota to fund a Center for Cannabis Research.
notes: From Minnesota H.F. 100 (2023)

claim: This report was informed by conversations with more than 60 stakeholders conducted over three months
context: This policy report was informed by conversations with more than 60 stakeholders conducted over three months, from August until October 2024.

claim: Nevada's cannabis consumption lounges first opened in early 2024, with several owned by social equity businesses
context: Nevada adopted laws authorizing cannabis consumption lounges in 2021. After a long and robust public process to develop rules, and for licensees to build out compliant locations, the first lounges opened in early 2024. Several are owned by social equity businesses.

claim: Oregon, California, and Washington have adopted laws authorizing interstate cannabis trade between consenting legalized states
context: State legislatures in Oregon (2019), California (2022) and Washington (2023) have adopted nearly identical laws authorizing trade between consenting legalized states. Under these frameworks, states would enter into agreements with each other authorizing distribution across state borders. None of these three states have entered into an interstate agreement with another state yet.
notes: None of these states had entered into an agreement yet as of December 2024

claim: Nevada retail prices for flower fell about 10% and edibles fell about 10% over 2023; concentrates fell about 4% and pre-rolls fell about 1%
context: Over the course of 2023, Nevada retail prices for flower and edibles fell by about 10%, concentrates fell by about 4%, and pre-rolls fell by about 1%.
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: Nevada flower retail prices in April and May 2024 were down more than 21% year-over-year vs same months in 2023
context: As shown in Table 1, over the first five months of 2024, the decrease in flower prices accelerated such that prices in April and May 2024 were down more than 21% year-over-year (vs. April and May 2023).
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: One-year retail price changes January-May 2024 vs same months in 2023 across product categories
context: Table 1. One-year retail price changes, January–May 2024. Difference between each month's average retail price and the retail price on the same month in 2023.
notes: Source: Author calculations using anonymized data from state Metrc track-and-trace system

claim: Ordinary flower retail prices declined from $6.18 per gram in May 2023 to $5.11 per gram in May 2024, a decline of about 17%
context: Ordinary flower prices declined from $6.18 per gram in May 2023 to $5.11 per gram in May 2024, a decline of about 17%.
notes: Linear decline of about 9.5 cents/month, R-squared = 0.98

claim: Solvent-based concentrate retail prices decreased about 27% between May 2023 and May 2024
context: Retail prices for solvent-based concentrates, a category that includes the THC distillates that make up most of the vape market, have been falling even more rapidly. Solvent-based retail prices decreased about 27% between May 2023 and May 2024.
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: Cannabis flower accounts for roughly 45% of the Nevada market by both volume (grams total THC) and revenue (retail dollars)
context: Cannabis flower accounts for roughly 45% of the Nevada market, whether measured by volume (in terms of grams total THC) or revenue (in retail dollars spent).
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: Pre-rolls have about 8% of Nevada market by volume and 12% by revenue; concentrates about half by volume and one-third by revenue; edibles about 2% by volume and 12% by revenue
context: Figure 5 shows the comparison for pre-rolls (the other dried-flower category), which have about 8% of the market by volume and 12% of the market by revenue. Figure 6 shows the same comparison for concentrates, which have about half the market by volume and about one-third by revenue. Figure 7 shows the comparison for edibles, which have about 2% of the market by volume and 12% by revenue.
notes: Volume measured in grams total THC

claim: Average wholesale flower prices in Nevada averaged about $1,350 per pound and fell to about $1,270 in December 2024
context: Average wholesale flower prices averaged about $1,350 per pound and fell throughout 2023, to about $1,270 in December 2024.
notes: Based on anonymized CCB METRC track-and-trace data

claim: Nevada wholesale prices were more than double California median prices; wholesale unpackaged flower prices declined almost 40% in 2023
context: Nevada's wholesale average was high for the U.S. western states in 2023, more than double California median prices. In 2023, wholesale prices declined most for unpackaged flower (almost 40%). Prices decreased by about 12% for packaged flower, and prices decreased only slightly for pre-rolls.
notes: Based on Metrc track-and-trace data

claim: Nevada wholesale-to-retail markups and average prices by product type, January-May 2024
